coredata.dc:title: Simplifying the complexity: Single enzyme (choline oxidase) inhibition-based biosensor with dual-readout method for organophosphorus pesticide detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Ziyu
Peng, Zoujun
Lai, Juanhua
Xu, Peng
Qiu, Ping
coredata.dc:description: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are widely used in agricultural production, but their residues could cause pollution to the environment and living organisms. In this paper, a simple dual-readout method for OPs detection was proposed based on ChOx single enzyme inhibition. Firstly, ChOx can catalyze the production of H2O2 from choline chloride (Ch-Cl). Bifunctional iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) with good peroxidase-like activity and superior fluorescence properties can catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) by H2O2 formed, and oxTMB could quench the fluorescence of Fe-CDs. In light of the fact that OPs exhibited activity in inhibiting ChOx, less H2O2 and the decreasing oxTMB led to a result that the fluorescence of the system recovered and the solution became lighter in blue color. Moreover, the process of ChOx inhibition by OPs was analyzed by molecular docking technique and it was found that OPs interact with key amino acid residues catalyzed by ChOx (Asn510, His466, Ser101, His351, Phe357, Trp331, Glu312). Finally, a dual-mode (colorimetry and fluorescence) sensor was created for the detection of OPs with the detection limit of 6 ng/L, and was successfully used in the quantitative determination of OPs in actual samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organophosphorus pesticides
Colorimetry
Fluorescence
Bifunctional carbon dots
Choline oxidase
Molecular dock

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dual-readout assays for determination of PM
Detection of PM in actual samples
Analysis of Fe-CDs like peroxidase activity
Sensing mechanism for detection of OPs
Analysis of ChOx inhibition by OPs
Detection of PM by dual-mode sensor
Determination of PM content in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21765015 , 21808099 ) and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province ( 20192BCD40001 ), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and size distribution of Fe-CDs. (B) High-resolution TEM image of Fe-CDs. (C) FT-IR spectra of Fe-CDs. (D) XPS spectra of the Fe-CDs and high-resolution C 1s (E), N 1s (F), O 1s (G), Fe 2p (H) Optical properties of Fe-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra: Fe-CDs (black curve), Fe-CDs and oxTMB (red curve). (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of oxTMB, excitation and emission spectrum of Fe-CDs. (C) The zeta potentials of Fe-CDs. (D) The fluorescence lifetime of Fe-CDs with and without oxTMB. (E) Stern-Volmer plot for fluorescein.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and (B) Fluorescence spectra of different mixed solutions. Conditions: 20 μL 0.75 μg/mL PM, 10 μL 0.1 mg/mL ChOx, 50 μL 4 mM Ch-Cl, 50 μL 0.4 mg/mL Fe-CDs, 50 μL 20 mM TMB, 100 μL pH = 7.6 PB, 740 μL pH = 5 HAc-NaAc buffer.
The binding mode of ChOx with PM. (A) The 3D structure of complex; (B) The 3D representation of stable conformation generated; (C) The 2D representation of stable conformation generated.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra with varying PM concentration (PM concentration from top to bottom. 0.06, 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 ng/mL), and the inset is optical images of the mixture as the PM concentration increases and (B) the calibration curve. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra with varying PM concentration (PM concentration from top to bottom. 25, 20, 15, 10, 5, 3, 0.8, 0.02 ng/mL) and (D) the calibration curve.
(A) The relative absorbance of the six OPs (All OPs concentrations were 80 nM). The selectivity of the detection system by (B) UV–vis and (C)Fluorescence.
Schematic diagram of a biosensor based on ChOx nhibition and Fe-CDs for OPs detection.
List of interaction parameters generated after docking of choline oxidase with PM.
The amount of PM in the actual samples was determined by colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode sensor.

head.ce:title: Simplifying the complexity: Single enzyme (choline oxidase) inhibition-based biosensor with dual-readout method for organophosphorus pesticide detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Center of Medical Device Testing, Nanchang, 330047, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Center of Medical Device Testing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Analysis and Testing, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Analysis and Testing, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are widely used in agricultural production, but their residues could cause pollution to the environment and living organisms. In this paper, a simple dual-readout method for OPs detection was proposed based on ChOx single enzyme inhibition. Firstly, ChOx can catalyze the production of H2O2 from choline chloride (Ch-Cl). Bifunctional iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) with good peroxidase-like activity and superior fluorescence properties can catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) by H2O2 formed, and oxTMB could quench the fluorescence of Fe-CDs. In light of the fact that OPs exhibited activity in inhibiting ChOx, less H2O2 and the decreasing oxTMB led to a result that the fluorescence of the system recovered and the solution became lighter in blue color. Moreover, the process of ChOx inhibition by OPs was analyzed by molecular docking technique and it was found that OPs interact with key amino acid residues catalyzed by ChOx (Asn510, His466, Ser101, His351, Phe357, Trp331, Glu312). Finally, a dual-mode (colorimetry and fluorescence) sensor was created for the detection of OPs with the detection limit of 6 ng/L, and was successfully used in the quantitative determination of OPs in actual samples with satisfactory results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the advancement of modern agriculture, the use of pesticides on crops has become a necessary stage in crop production [1]. Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are by far one of the most extensively used pesticides in the world [2]. Although OPs are frequently employed to manage pests in rice, wheat, vegetables, fruit, cotton, and pastures [1], they can also easily linger in food, agricultural goods, and environmental samples [3]. As a result of the extensive use of organophosphorus insecticides, significant OPs contamination of aquatic organisms' environments can result in their demise [4]. On the other hand, OPs possess acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitory activities and further enter the human body through environmental accumulation in food and drinking water, causing health problems such as dizziness, vomiting, central nervous system toxicity, and respiratory paralysis. Besides, OPs disrupt the growth-promoting mechanisms of plants by inhibiting a variety of enzymes necessary for their growth, and reduce the quality of microbial communities and soil fertility [4–7]. Pesticide residues are governed by a number of nations and organizations around the world, with 0.01 mg/kg being the maximum residual limit listed for fruits and vegetables in the pesticide database of the European Commission (EC) [8]. Therefore, it is necessary to establish a rapid and reliable method for the detection of OPs.

Various methods for the detection of OPs have been proposed such as gas chromatography (GC) [9], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) [11], and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [12]. These methods are sensitive enough for the detection of OPs, but there may be the shortcoming of the existing methods, including more time consuming, tedious, laborious, and require expensive instruments as well as complicated procedures for their use. In contrast, colorimetric and fluorescent methods based on enzyme inhibition could be explored to detect OPs indirectly through color or fluorescence signals without involving complex preparation processes. The biosensors for detecting OPs could be developed based on directly use natural enzymes, with the advantages of high sensitivity, short response time, simple operation, and good selectivity [13–15]. Carbon dots (CDs)-based nanoenzymes have shown great potential for biomedical and environmental applications due to their biocompatibility, high catalytic activity, and simple surface functionalization. And they also possess unique optical properties which could provide a versatile platform for the construction of biosensors [16,17]. Most methods for OPs were built on the basis of enzyme inhibition and peroxidase-like activity of the material, catalyzing the oxidation of chromophores (TMB, OPD, ABTS) to produce color signals or fluorescent signals [18–20]. The mechanism is based on dual enzyme method that OPs possess the inhibitory activity on AChE and acetylthiocholine (ATCh) could be catalyzed by ChOx to produce H2O2. However, it has been found that OPs have an inhibitory effect on ChOx activity [21–23]. Obviously, the ChOx single enzyme method is cheaper and simpler to operate than the dual enzyme method of AChE-ChOx, but currently these methods have problems such as single detection signal, low detection sensitivity, or narrow detection range [21–23]. In the AChE single enzyme method, the signal conversion usually depends on enzymatic hydrolysis products, such as thiocholine. However, these products can easily be degraded and oxidized in real environments, resulting in inaccurate or false positive results [24]. And the enzyme-free method lacks enough selective recognition on OPs. Based on this, we developed a sensitive and reliable double readout assay (colorimetry and fluorescence) for the detection of OPs using a ChOx single-enzyme method.

Molecular docking approach is the effective computer modeling technology in the study of intramolecular and usually is applied to simulate the geometric structure and intermolecular forces of molecules utilizing chemometrics and other disciplines. It is possible to predict the structure of receptor-ligand complexes and analyze the interactions between ligands and their receptors to infer their binding modes and affinity. In this study, iron-doped carbon dots (Fe-CDs) were synthesized with good peroxidase-like activity and bright blue fluorescence. ChOx catalyzes the hydrolysis of choline chloride (Ch-Cl) to create H2O2 and Fe-CDs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to obtain oxTMB, which could quench the blue fluorescence of Fe-CDs. However, the quantity of H2O2 would reduce owing to the inhibition activity of OPs on ChOx. As a result, the UV–vis absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm got weak and the fluorescence of Fe-CDs at 470 nm got strong with the increasing concentration of OPs. A double reading approach (colorimetric and fluorescence) was developed according to the sensor principle. Furthermore, the molecular docking technique was chosen to explore the inhibition activity mechanism of OPs on ChOx, utilizing parathion-methyl (PM) as a representative model. We discovered that PM interacted with important amino acid residues (Asn510, His466, Ser101, His351, Phe357, Trp331, Glu312) catalyzed by ChOx.

All the chemical substances used were analytical grade reagents. The parathion-methyl (PM) standard with acetone as solvent at a concentration of 10 mg/L was purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Choline oxidase (ChOx, 8–20 U/mg, 50 U), choline chloride (Ch-Cl), citric acid (CA), 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) purchased from Macklin Biologicals Biochemical Ltd Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Choline chloride dissolved in ultrapure water should be prepared when using. Ferric chloride was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) and ethylenediamine (EDA) was obtained from Titan Scientific Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The water used in the experiment was ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm, Millipore, USA). The following buffer solutions were used in this work: phosphate buffer (PBS) (pH = 7.4; 20 mM) for the configuration of cholinesterase solution; phosphate buffer (pH = 7.6; 10 mM); acetic acid sodium acetate buffer (HAc-NaAc) (pH = 5.0; 0.1 M).

Fe-CDs were synthesized by the previously reported one-pot hydrothermal method [25] with some changes in reaction conditions. Herein, 0.9605 g of citric acid and 0.8110 g of ferric chloride were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, then 0.67 mL of ethylenediamine was added dropwise, stirred for 10 min and put into a reaction kettle at 180 °C for 10 h. After the reaction was finished, the reaction solution was naturally cooled to room temperature. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to remove the insoluble precipitate, and the supernatant was dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. The purified reaction solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and finally the solution was lyophilized to obtain Fe-CDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-readout assays for determination of PM
ce:section.ce:para: 20 μL of varying concentrations of PM, together with 10 μL of 0.1 mg/mL ChOx, were incubated for 10 min at 25 °C. 50 μL of 4 mM Ch-Cl solution and 100 μL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.6) were added to the mixed solution and incubated for 30 min at 25 °C. Next, 720 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 5.0), 50 μL of 20 mM TMB solution and 50 μL of 0.4 mg/mL Fe-CDs solution was continued to mix with the reaction solution and was incubated at 45 °C for 20 min. Finally both the fluorescence and UV–vis signals were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PM in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: 10 g of samples (cabbage, leek, pear) from local market were mashed and soaked in 20.0 mL of acetone for 24 h. The supernatant was extracted, and the filtrate was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble material. The filtrate was mixed with different concentrations of PM (2, 10, 20 ng/mL), and diluted 20 times. Colorimetric-fluorescence dual-readout assays for PM was carried out by the above-mentioned process.

The crystal structure of ChOx (PDBID: 4MJW) was obtained from the Protein Data Bank (https://www.rcsb.org/). After removing extraneous small molecules from the protein molecules using PyMol 2.1 open source software [26], the protein molecules were imported into AutoDock Tools 4.2 open source software [27] to remove water molecules, then add hydrogen atoms and set the atom types. Finally they were saved as pdbqt files. The chemical structures of PM was obtained from the PubChem database. PM was utilized as a small molecule ligand, while ChOx protein of post-treatment was employed as an acceptor. The center position of the Grid Box (x = −44.878, y = −8.545, z = −18.296) was determined from the natural ligand of the target (BEL). The dimensions were set to 40 Å × 40 Å × 50 Å and the grid distance was set to 0.375 Å. Molecular docking was performed by AutoDock 4.2 to analyze the results, and the binding interaction of compounds to proteins was visualized using PyMol 2.1 software. During the calculations, the Lamarckian genetic algorithm was introduced for molecular docking calculations with the following algorithm: a population of 150, a maximum of 25 million energy evaluations, a maximum number of 2000, a crossover rate of 0.8, a mutation rate of 0.02, 100 independent docking runs and the final docking structure was evaluated according to the binding free energy [28].

The interaction between OPs and ChOx was analyzed, including the resulting hydrogen bonding, π-π interactions, and hydrophobic interactions. This analysis offered valuable insights into the inhibition of ChOx activity by OPs.

Firstly, we characterized the morphology of the prepared Fe-CDs by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, the prepared Fe-CDs were homogeneous and spherical, and their average particle size was about 3.56 nm as shown by the histogram of particle size distribution (Fig. 1A inset), which was similar to the particle size of the carbon dots synthesized in the reference literature [25]. Fig. 1B suggests the lattice size of 0.23 nm, attributed to the (100) lattice face of graphite carbon [29]. The composition of Fe-CDs was analyzed using FT-IR to determine the main components (Fig. 1C). The wide peak within 3200–3600 cm−1 demonstrates the presence of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, and the band at 2810 cm−1 corresponds to the C–H stretching vibration [25]. Moreover, the FT-IR spectra revealed the stretching vibrations of CO/CN [30] at 1631 cm−1, C–N–C [31] at 1352 cm−1, C–O–C [25] at 1127 cm−1, C–O [32] at 1038 cm−1, Fe–N [32] at 763 cm−1, and Fe–O [33] at 622 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to investigate the elemental composition of Fe-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1D, the spectra of Fe-CDs include C 1s (284.76 eV), N 1s (400.04 eV), O 1s (530.89 eV) and Fe 2p (710.46 eV), and the elemental composition was calculated as 62.74% carbon, 19.09% nitrogen, 16.04% oxygen and 2.13% iron, indicating the successful doping of iron atoms into the CDs. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of Fe-CDs (Fig. 1E) confirmed the existence of C–C (284.58 eV), C–N (285.58 eV), C–O (286.88 eV), and CO (287.48 eV) [34]. The presence of pyridinic-N (399.98 eV) and pyrrolic-N (400.58 eV) was confirmed by the high-resolution N 1s spectral analysis of Fe-CDs (Fig. 1F). The high-resolution O 1s spectra of Fe-CDs (Fig. 1G) proved the presence of Fe–O (530.78 eV) and CO (531.98 eV) [25]. The high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 1H) indicated two pairs of peaks attributed to Fe 2p3/2 (710.48 eV) and Fe 2p1/2 (723.58 eV), where the Fe2+ signal close to 711 eV is due to the presence of the Fe-Nx site [29]. All the characterization results show that we have successfully prepared Fe-CDs.

The fluorescence properties of Fe-CDs were investigated in details. As shown in Fig. S1A, the different fluorescence emission intensity can be observed at varying excitation wavelengths between 310 and 400 nm, and the maximum intensity can be obtained at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. At the same time, we also found that a red shift of the emission peaks took place with the increase of the excitation wavelength, showing the dependence of the emission wavelength on the excitation wavelength [35]. That was thought to be caused by different conjugation lengths of carbon nuclei or different surface functional groups, indicating that Fe-CDs may have multiple photoluminescence centers, which is consistent with the general characteristics of most carbon dots [36]. And, Fig. S1B shows the UV–vis absorption spectra (red curve), emission spectra (blue curve) and excitation spectra (black curve) of Fe-CDs measured in aqueous solution. There are two UV–vis absorption peaks detected at 239 nm and 345 nm, which should be from π-π* leaps in the sp2 orbital of the CO group and π-π* leaps in the sp2 orbital of the CN group, respectively [37]. From the inset of Fig. S1B, it can be seen that the synthesized Fe-CDs aqueous solution is pale yellow in visible light and emits bright blue fluorescence under the UV–vis lamp at 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Fe-CDs like peroxidase activity
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase catalytic activity of Fe-CDs was evaluated using TMB as a substrate (Fig. S2). In the presence of H2O2, Fe-CDs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB to produce blue oxTMB (Fig. S2 inset), accompanied by a characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm (red curve). In contrast, the control group (Fe-CDs + TMB) showed neither obvious color change nor characteristic peak at 652 nm, which indicates that Fe-CDs have peroxidase-like activity and can catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2.

The steady-state kinetics of TMB oxidation catalyzed by Fe-CDs was investigated at room temperature and the peroxidase-like activity of Fe-CDs was also further explored by means of enzyme kinetic theory. We analyzed the variation of the absorbance (652 nm) of the UV–vis spectra with the reaction time under the constant TMB concentration and different H2O2 levels (Fig. S3A). The Michaelis constants (K
m ) and maximum reaction velocities (V max) could be calculated by Michaelis-Menten Equation S(1) and Lineweaver-Burk Equation S(2).

Base on this, their Lineweaver-Burk plots (Fig. S3B) can be obtained, and the calculated K
m and V max were 0.869 mM and 45.46 × 10−8 M/s, respectively. A smaller K
m indicates a higher affinity of the material for H2O2 binding, while a larger V max indicates a higher catalytic activity of the material. On the other hand, we kept the sensor at constant H2O2 concentration, and the absorbance of the UV–vis spectrum (at 652 nm) was investigated at different TMB levels varied with the reaction time (Fig. S3C). The responding Lineweaver-Burk plot (Fig. S3D) could be also achieved, and the relevant K
m and V max were evaluated for 0.395 mM and 3.87 × 10−8 M/s, respectively. Comparison of the kinetic parameters of Fe-CDs in this work with those of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), Fe-CDs synthesized by other method and other nanoenzymes (Table S1), it was included that the Fe-CDs synthesized have good catalytic activity and better binding ability to TMB and H2O2.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence of Fe-CDs at 470 nm dropped considerably when oxTMB was introduced, indicating the fluorescence quenching by oxTMB. To investigate the fluorescence mechanism of, the UV–vis absorption peak of oxTMB at 370 nm was first measured and it was found there was overlap with both the excitation peak (350 nm) and the emission peak (470 nm) of Fe-CDs (Fig. 2B), inferring that the quench mechanism was related to the internal filtration effect (IFE) or energy resonance transfer (FRET) [38]. The zeta potential results of Fe-CDs show that Fe-CDs are positively charged (Fig. 2C) and oxTMB is also positively charged [39], so there is electrostatic repulsion between Fe-CDs and oxTMB, and no FRET occurs at intermolecular distances exceeding 10 nm [38]. In Fig. 2D, the fluorescence lifetime of Fe-CDs decreased from 14.84 ns to 12.31 ns after the addition of oxTMB. As we know, IFE does not affect the fluorescence lifetime, which means that there is another fluorescence quenching mechanism, presumably a dynamic quenching effect (DQE) that may affect the fluorescence lifetime [40]. Stern-Volmer plot about fluorescence quenching of Fe-CDs by oxTMB was shown in Fig. 2E, in which F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs before and after fluorescence quenching, respectively, and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher. The non-linearity of the Stern-Volmer plot indicates that combined DQE and static quenching effect (SQE) are involved [41]. In summary oxTMB quenches the fluorescence of Fe-CDs as a combined quench of IFE, DQE, and SQE.

Under the catalysis of Fe-CDs, H2O2 can oxidize TMB to oxTMB, which would quench the fluorescence of Fe-CDs. With the increase of H2O2 concentration, the UV–vis absorbance of the reaction solution at 652 nm increased linearly (Fig. S4A), while the fluorescence emission intensity at 470 nm decreased linearly (Fig. S4C). As shown in Fig. S4B, the absorbance at 652 nm was linearly correlated with the H2O2 concentration at 1–45 μM with a linear regression equation of A = 0.0203C + 0.0934 (R2 = 0.997), and the LOD was calculated as 0.28 μM based on 3σ/s (s is the slope of the equation, σ is the standard deviation). As shown in Fig. S4D, the fluorescence emission peak at 470 nm was linearly correlated with the H2O2 concentration at 1–20 μM with a linear regression equation of F = −123.64C + 5309.0 (R2 = 0.992) and the LOD of 0.31 μM.

As demonstrated in Equation S(3) and S(4), ChOx catalyzes choline to glycine betaine and H2O2 through a two-step catalysis with betaine aldehyde as an intermediate product. As we discussed earlier, Fe-CDs was applied to assay H2O2 combined with TMB, so ChOx can be detected through quantitative analysis of H2O2 according to the same principle. As shown in Fig. S5A, the characteristic peak at 652 nm was not observed in both the solution of Fe-CDs alone and the mixed solution of TMB and Fe-CDs (curve d and e), and individual ChOx or Ch-Cl could not oxidize TMB catalyzed by Fe-CDs (curve b and c). Only the mixed reaction solution of ChOx, Ch-Cl, TMB, and Fe-CDs could produce blue oxTMB, with an UV–vis absorption peak at 652 nm (curve a). As shown in Fig. S5B, the Fe-CDs have a symmetric fluorescence emission peak at 470 nm (curve d), and all of TMB, ChOx, and Ch-Cl did not affect the Fe-CDs fluorescence (curve b, c, and e). However, the fluorescence intensity of Fe-CDs was quenched when ChOx, Ch-Cl, TMB and Fe-CDs were all present (curve a). Therefore, this method was feasible to detection of ChOx.

We explored the linear range and detection limits of the assay to demonstrate the performance of the method for ChOx quantification. As shown in the inset of Fig. S5C, the color of the mixed solution gradually deepened from left to right as the concentration of ChOx increased, and the absorbance at 652 nm also increased accordingly. The calibration curve of UV–vis absorbance at 652 nm versus ChOx concentration was obtained in the range of 0.01–0.6 μg/mL (Fig. S5D), and the linear regression equation was A = 2.2571CChOx + 0.0735 (R2 = 0.991) with the LOD of 2.6 ng/mL. As shown in Fig. S5E, the degree of fluorescence quench deepened as the ChOx concentration increased. The fluorescence intensity at 470 nm showed a good linear correlation with the corresponding ChOx concentration in the range of 0.02–0.24 μg/mL (Fig. S5F), and the linear regression equation was F = −10841.30CChOx + 4393.07 (R2 = 0.993) with the LOD of 5.2 ng/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism for detection of OPs
ce:section.ce:para: In Scheme 1 , Ch-Cl could be catalyzed by ChOx to generate H2O2. Meanwhile, Fe-CDs have both peroxidase activity and blue fluorescence emission. H2O2 can oxidize TMB to blue oxTMB under the catalysisof Fe-CDs, and the resulting oxTMB quenched the fluorescence of Fe-CDs. Therefore, when OPs was introduced, OPs would inhibit the activity of ChOx. As a result, the higher OPs concentration, the weaker the ChOx activity, and the less H2O2 was generated. Eventually, the level of oxTMB obtained by oxidation decreased with the fading blue solution, while the fluorescence recovery of Fe-CDs was observed, which formed the concept for dual-mode (colorimetric and fluorescent) OPs sensing.

In order to verify the above-mentioned mechanism, we conducted a feasibility investigation. In this work, parathion-methyl (PM) was selected as representative of OPs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when ChOx, Ch-Cl, TMB and Fe-CDs were present simultaneously, a distinct UV–vis absorption peak (curve a) appeared at 652 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrogram, which is apparently the characteristic absorption peak of oxTMB. The absence of this absorption peak in curves c, d, and f indicated that no oxTMB was produced in these three systems, which is because that neither ChOx or Ch-Cl participated in the oxidation of TMB to oxTMB, and Fe-CDs could not oxidize TMB directly. Predictably, the absorbance of the system at 652 nm decreased (curve b) when PM got involved in the system, indicating that colorimetric detection of PM was proved to be practicable. On the other side, as shown in Fig. 3B, Fe-CDs had a intense fluorescence emission peak at 470 nm (curve e), and the fluorescence emission peak did not change significantly when Fe-CDs were mixed with the combination of ChOx, Ch-Cl, and TMB alone (curves c, d, and f). The fluorescence was quenched when ChOx, Ch-Cl, TMB, and Fe-CDs were mixed at the same time (curve a), but the fluorescence partially recovered after introducing PM into the mixture (curve b). Therefore, this system can be used for the detection of PM by colorimetric-fluorescence double reading method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ChOx inhibition by OPs
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the potential interaction sites between ChOx and OPs, we chose PM as the ligand for molecular docking calculations, and the results are shown in Table 1 . Blind docking of PM and ChOx was performed using Autodock software, and the most stable conformation was selected for further interpretation based on the best energy ordering. The conformation with a binding free energy of −5.88 kcal/mol was chosen with an inhibition constant of 48.65 μM. It was reported that the docking free energy of ChOx with paraoxon methyl (POM) was −4.39 kcal/mol with an inhibition constant of 608.6 μM [23]. Both PM and POM inhibit the activity of ChOx, but the inhibition constant value of PM and ChOx is smaller indicating that PM has a stronger ability to inhibit ChOx.

In this experiment, PM was molecularly docked with codA (ChOx). The molecular docking results showed that PM combines ChOx well and have a good match. The docked compound-protein complex was visualized using Pymol 2.1 software to obtain the binding pattern of the compound to the protein (Fig. 4 A), and the amino acid residues bound to the protein pocket could be clearly seen based on the binding pattern (Fig. 4B). PM can bind in the vicinity of the catalytic active center of ChOx. To further understand the binding of the compound to the protein pocket, we performed an in-depth analysis of the binding mode (Fig. 4C). It was included that PM can have strong hydrogen bonding interactions with Asn510, His466, Ser101, Thr463 amino acids, and the benzene ring portion can form π-π conjugation interactions with His351. The positively charged portion can form π-π conjugate interactions with Phe357, Trp331, Glu312, and these interactions can effectively promote PM binding to ChOx.

Many researchers have investigated the mechanism of ChOx catalytic choline in detail. Rungsrisuriyachai et al. demonstrated that Asn510 has an important role in ChOx catalytic choline oxidation by exploring the mechanism of Asn510 in ChOx catalytic oxidation process [42]. It was also reported that the treated His466 mutant ChOx was 45-fold less active than the wild-type ChOx [43], so it can be assumed that His466 plays an important role in ChOx catalysis. Moreover, it was discovered that Ser101 is a crucial residue in catalytic ChOx, playing a key role in activating the alcohol substrate and facilitating the transfer of hydride ions from activated alkanes to flavins [44]. The docking results of PM with ChOx showed that Asn510, His466, Ser101 amino acid residues have strong hydrogen bonding interactions with PM, so PM may inhibit the catalytic activity of ChOx through hydrogen bonding interactions on these amino acid residues. On the other hand, the benzene ring of PM forms π-π conjugation with His351, and it was confirmed that His351 contributes to the binding and localization of the substrate and stabilizes the transition state of the hydride to flavin transfer reaction [45], which is an important active site involved in ChOx catalysis. Gating of ChOx catalytic activity occurs mainly through hydrophobic interactions between the side chain clusters of five amino acid residues (Met62, Leu65, Val355, Phe357, and Met359). The side chains of Trp61, Trp331, and Phe357 form a hydrophobic cage on the surface of the active site near the isoxopyrimidine ring of flavin [46]. The residues, along with the negatively charged side chain of Glu312, form the interaction site for the trimethylamine moiety of choline. In addition, it is particularly important that the side chain of Glu312 forms the “top” of the aromatic cage and is the only negatively charged residue present in the active site cavity [47]. Therefore, all the amino acid residues of Phe357, Trp331, and Glu312 play important roles in the process of ChOx-catalyzed Ch-Cl, and the positively charged part of PM forms Cation-π conjugation interactions with this amino acid residue.

Thus, the above analysis results of the molecular docking study suggests that PM interacts with a variety of key residues of ChOx that are responsible for the enzymatic activity of ChOx. PM may inhibit the enzymatic activity of ChOx by affecting these amino acid residues, which is consistent with the experimental results of inhibiting the catalytic efficiency of the ChOx enzyme in the presence of PM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PM by dual-mode sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The linear range and detection limit of PM were investigated under the optimal experimental conditions to demonstrate the performance of this fluorescent colorimetric dual-mode sensor for PM. With the increase of PM concentration, the color of the reaction system gradually became lighter (inset of Fig. 5 A), and the UV–vis absorbance at 652 nm decreased accordingly (Fig. 5A), while the intensity of the fluorescence emission at 470 nm gradually increased (Fig. 5C).

In Fig. 5B, the UV–vis absorbance at 652 nm was linearly correlated with the PM concentration in the range of 0.06–25 ng/mL, and the linear regression equation obtained was A = −0.01536CPM + 0.65338 (R2 = 0.996), with the LOD of 43 ng/L. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity at 470 nm was linearly correlated with the PM concentration in the range of 0.02–25 ng/mL (Fig. 5D), with a linear regression equation of F = 62.666CPM + 2103.186 (R2 = 0.995), and the LOD of 6 ng/L was obtained. The present method was compared with other methods for the detection of OPs in recent years, and Table S2 was compiled. The present method based on ChOx nhibition and Fe-CDs simplified the complexity and has a high sensitivity and a good detection range.

In order to verify the response of this method for various OPs, several pesticides (isocarbophos, profenofos, parathion, sulfotep, plepthion) were selected for detection by this method. The relative absorbance intensity (A0-A)/A0 was applied to evaluate selectivity, in which A0 is the absorbance before the addition of OPs, and A is the absorbance intensity after the addition of OPs. As showed in Fig. 6 A, PM showed the strongest inhibition of ChOx activity at the same concentration, and the other five OPs showed a degree of inhibition of ChOx activity, which also illustrates the universality of this method for the detection of OPs.

To evaluate the immunity of the proposed colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode sensor to the quantitative detection of OPs in real samples, the selectivity results are shown in Fig. 6B and C. We selected common interfering ions and substances in the actual sample (cabbage, leek, pear), such as metal ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, NH4 +, Cl−, HPO4 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, H2PO4 −), biomolecules (urea, glycine, glutamic acid, riboflavin). The concentration of OPs was kept at 3 ng/mL, and the concentration of metal ions and urea, glycine (Gly) and glutamic acid (Glu) was set to 100 times the concentration of OPs (3 μg/mL), and the results show that no interference was observed from these substances. Actually, riboflavin (VB2) had fluorescence properties and could interfere with the fluorescence measurement at higher concentration. However, when the VB2 concentration fell to 20 times (0.6 μg/mL), no significant effect was found.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PM content in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to verify the practicality of this colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode sensor, we selected leek, cabbage and pear as the study objects because the leek had the highest OPs residues in vegetables. As showed in Table 2 , the recoveries (n = 3) in the actual samples ranged from 87.0 to 118.0% with standard deviations less than 5.1%, indicating that the method can be applied to the detection of PM in actual samples. Furthermore, the obtained results using colorimetric-fluorescence dual mode sensor were analyzed with F test. The values measured by the F test (F S
2
1 /S
2
2 , and S is the standard deviation) were all smaller than the F critical value (19.00) from the F table [48], indicating that there was no significant difference in precision between the UV–vis method and the fluorescence method at a confidence level of 95%.

The single enzyme method based on ChOx inhibition was proposed to assay OPs combined with Fe-CDs and TMB by colorimetric-fluorescence dual signal. The mechanism of ChOx inhibition by OPs was analyzed through molecular docking approach and it was included that OPs interact with key amino acid residues catalyzed by ChOx (Asn510, His466, Ser101, His351, Phe357, Trp331, Glu312). The detection of OPs using H2O2 as a mediator had to utilize both AChE and ChOx, which affected ChOx catalyzed choline production of H2O2 through the inhibition of AChE by OPs. By comparison, this proposed assay has the benefit of simplifying the complexity and offering a clear version for the mechanism, which was successfully applied to the detection of OPs residues in real samples. It has a great application in the detection of OPs, and provides a new way to combine nanoenzyme for multiple signals to detect OPs.
Ziyu Yan: Investigation, Writing - Original Draft. Zoujun Peng: Methodology. Juanhua Lai: Validation. Peng Xu: Data Curation. Ping Qiu: Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21765015, 21808099) and the Science and Technology Innovation Platform of Jiangxi Province (20192BCD40001), China.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of molecular interactions and structural defects in the efficient fluorescence quenching by carbon nanotubes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.K. Singh, P.K. Iyer, P.K. Giri, Role of molecular interactions and structural defects in the efficient fluorescence quenching by carbon nanotubes, Carbon. 50 (2012) 4495-4505. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2012.05.030.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of asparagine 510 in the relative timing of substrate bond cleavages in the reaction catalyzed by choline oxidase
Biochemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Rungsrisuriyachai, G. Gadda, Role of asparagine 510 in the relative timing of substrate bond cleavages in the reaction catalyzed by choline oxidase, Biochemistry. 49 (2010) 2483-2490. https://doi.org/10.1021/bi901796a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On the catalytic role of the conserved active site residue His 466 of choline oxidase
Biochemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ghanem, G. Gadda, On the catalytic role of the conserved active site residue His 466 of choline oxidase, Biochemistry. 44 (2005) 893-904. https://doi.org/10.1021/bi048056j.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Importance of a serine proximal to the C(4a) and N(5) flavin atoms for hydride transfer in choline oxidase
Biochemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Yuan, G. Gadda, Importance of a serine proximal to the C(4a) and N(5) flavin atoms for hydride transfer in choline oxidase, Biochemistry. 50 (2011) 770-779. https://doi.org/10.1021/bi101837u.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On the role of Histidine 351 in the reaction of alcohol oxidation catalyzed by choline oxidase
Biochemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Rungsrisuriyachai, G. Gadda, On the role of Histidine 351 in the reaction of alcohol oxidation catalyzed by choline oxidase, Biochemistry. 47 (2008) 6762-6769. https://doi.org/10.1021/bi800650w.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The cluster of hydrophobic residues controls the entrance to the active site of choline oxidase
Biochemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xin, G. Gadda, D. Hamelberg, The cluster of hydrophobic residues controls the entrance to the active site of choline oxidase, Biochemistry. 48 (2009) 9599-9605. https://doi.org/10.1021/bi901295a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of Glu312 in binding and positioning of the substrate for the hydride transfer reaction in choline oxidase
Biochemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Quaye, G.T. Lountos, Fan, A.M. Orville, G. Gadda, Role of Glu312 in binding and positioning of the substrate for the hydride transfer reaction in choline oxidase, Biochemistry. 47 (2008) 243-256. https://doi.org/10.1021/bi7017943.
